preFix.title: Air-dry clay fruit bowl with textured rim
preFix.subtitle: Wide shallow draped bowl with a rope-impressed textured border
preFix.excerpt: A wide shallow bowl formed by draping a rolled slab over an upturned bowl former, with a decorative rim pressed with twisted rope or jute cord before the clay stiffens. Approximately 200 mm across and 60 mm tall. No kiln is required. PVA sealed; suitable for displaying dry fruit.
preFix.sourceNotes: Technique derived from first principles. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) pottery entry consulted for historical context on slab-building and mould-draping methods.

content.text: This bowl is formed by the
drape-forming
method: a rolled
slab
is laid over an upturned bowl former and the rim is decorated with
impressed texture

from twisted rope or jute cord while the clay is still soft. No kiln is required. The finished bowl is approximately 200 mm across and 60 mm tall. It is suitable for displaying dry fruit at room temperature but is not a functional vessel for wet items or prolonged use with fresh produce.

materials.name: Twisted rope or jute cord (for rim texture)

content.text: Preparation

Select a round household bowl approximately 200 mm across the base when turned upside down. Cover its outer surface (which becomes the mould) with a sheet of cling film, pressing it smoothly against the surface. Prepare a 40 cm length of twisted rope or jute cord for the rim impression. Knead the working clay for two minutes to remove air pockets before rolling. Work on a wooden board lightly dusted with cornflour or covered with a cloth.
Construction

Roll the slab. Roll the clay to a consistent 6 mm thickness across a sheet at least 230 mm in diameter. Use guide rails on either side of the rolling pin to keep the thickness even. Roll from the centre outward, rotating the slab a quarter turn between each pass.

Cut the circle. Cut a circle 230 mm in diameter from the slab using the fettling knife and a card circle template (cut a circle from card or trace around a dinner plate of the right size). The extra 30 mm beyond the finished bowl diameter allows for the slab to drape down over the sides of the mould.

Drape the slab over the mould. Lay the clay circle centrally over the upturned cling-filmed bowl. Press the slab downward gently at the centre with the flat of your hand, then smooth outward toward the rim so the slab conforms to the bowl's curvature. Smooth the surface with a damp sponge. Check that the clay drapes evenly all around with no creases or air pockets between clay and mould.

Trim and form the rim. Use the fettling knife to trim around the clay edge at a consistent depth (approximately 60 mm below the bowl centre point measured along the side) to produce a clean, level rim. Remove the excess clay. Smooth the cut edge with a damp finger or sponge.

Add the rope texture to the rim immediately after trimming, while the clay is still fully soft. Lay the twisted rope or jute cord against the outer face of the rim and press it into the clay using firm, even finger pressure all the way around the circumference. The cord should leave a clear twisted-rope impression in the clay surface. Lift the cord away carefully.

Add any additional decoration. Use the needle tool to incise a border line 10 mm below the rope texture all the way around the rim. Alternatively, add a second rope impression or a stamp pattern in the smooth interior of the bowl.
Allow the bowl to reach the
leather-hard

stage on the mould. At this point the bowl will hold its shape when lifted off the mould. Peel away the cling film and place the bowl right-side up on a flat wooden board to finish drying. Check that the rim sits level.
Drying
Allow the bowl to dry at room temperature for 48-3 days until it reaches the
bone-dry
stage: uniformly pale and room temperature to the touch. The
greenware

bowl is fragile, particularly around the textured rim which has been thinned by the rope impression. Handle the bowl by supporting it from below and avoid pressing on the rim. Do not stack or invert the bowl during drying.
Finishing

Paint the bowl interior and exterior with acrylic paint if desired. A terracotta, cream, or natural earth tone complements the rope texture. Allow the paint to dry for at least two hours. Apply two coats of PVA diluted 50:50 with water to all surfaces, working the brush into the rope-texture impressions to ensure full coverage. Allow each coat to dry before applying the next. The rope texture traps the sealer well and the sealed impression reads clearly against the surrounding clay.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof even after PVA sealing. This bowl is not suitable as a water vessel and should not hold wet or damp produce for extended periods. It is suitable for displaying dry citrus, apples, or decorative gourds at room temperature. Keep indoors. Wipe with a dry cloth. Do not submerge in water, put in a dishwasher, or leave in a damp location.
Variations
Deeper bowl: use a deeper household bowl as the mould to produce a bowl 80-90 mm tall with higher sides, which holds small items as a desk or entryway catch-all.

Folded rim: fold the outer edge of the clay back on itself before draping, instead of leaving a flat trimmed rim. This creates a double-thickness rolled rim that takes the rope impression especially crisply.

Leaf-impressed interior: before draping, press large flat leaves (such as fatsia or fig leaves) textured-side down onto the slab surface, then roll lightly over them with the rolling pin. Remove the leaves before draping so the leaf veins are impressed into the interior of the bowl.

rows.fix: Lift the section with the crease, smooth the clay flat, then re-drape slowly from the centre outward. Work quickly before the clay stiffens
rows.cause: The circle was too large for the mould or the clay was draped unevenly
rows.problem: Slab develops creases when draped over the mould

rows.fix: At the soft stage, re-press the rope into the same impression with firmer, more even pressure. If the clay has already stiffened past the workable stage, use the needle tool to deepen the impression lines by hand
rows.cause: The rope was pressed with insufficient or uneven pressure
rows.problem: Rim texture is too faint or uneven

rows.fix: During the first 24 hours, lay a damp cloth strip loosely around the rim to slow that zone. At leather-hard, work slip into any crack and smooth over
rows.cause: The rim dried faster than the body due to being thinner after the rope impression

rows.fix: At leather-hard, press the base gently onto a flat board. If fully bone-dry, add a thin ring of air-dry clay to the underside as a foot ring, fixed with slip, to level the piece
rows.cause: The base of the bowl dried on the mould unevenly
rows.problem: Bowl does not sit flat and rocks when placed on a surface

rows.fix: Slide a thin palette knife between the clay and the mould surface in small increments around the circumference to break the bond. Peel away slowly
rows.cause: Cling film was not applied or had a gap
rows.problem: Bowl sticks to the mould when removed
